EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On January 14, 2024, a strong pressure gradient resulted over Southern Connecticut in part from a strong low pressure system slowly moving across Southeastern Canada. An associated cold front passed through during the afternoon. The combination of the cold front and strong pressure gradient helped produce high wind gusts across the area during the afternoon hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 60 mph occurred at a mesonet station at Stonington Outer Breakwater, CT at 249 PM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system impacted the area late on January 9th and into the 10th with long duration persistent moderate to heavy rainfall. In addition to widespread river flooding, some localized areas of flash flooding occurred as the heaviest rainfall associated with convection moved through. Precipitable water values of 1.2-1.4 inches were record levels for early January. The anomalous moisture and heavy rainfall resulted in a widespread 2 - 4 inches of rainfall, much of it occurring over a several hour timeframe before the cold frontal passage. The Yantic River reached Major Flood Stage on January 10th at 9Z and continued to remain in Major flood stage until 2330Z.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yantic River remained in Major Flood Stage with a reported partial dam break in Fitchville as a result of heavy rain and snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure moved northeast from the Mid-Atlantic Region to southern New England during the night of January 9, 2024 into the early morning hours of January 10, 2024. Its center passed just north of the region during this period. The pressure gradient tightened as the center of the storm approached, producing high winds over Southern Connecticut during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple high wind gusts were recorded at mesonet sites, including 59 mph at 1145 PM Local Standard Time in Fairfield, CT and 60 mph at 1026 PM Local Standard Time in Stamford at an elevation of 131 feet.

This occurred in New Haven, CT at 945 PM Local Standard Time on January 9, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick hitting winter storm brought around 12 hours of snow, centered over Connecticut, with the greatest amount of snow in Massachusetts falling on Cape Cod and the islands. This was a notoriously difficult to forecast storm passing south of 40/70 benchmark, which was a significant shift south in the final 24 hours leading up to the storm. This moved the band of heavy snow well south of Boston (0.1) to Cape Cod which received 7-9 inches of snow. The heaviest snow of the storm fell in Hartford County, Connecticut where over 15 inches of snow was reported. The result was many schools and businesses in eastern MA closing and receiving little to no snow. Cape Cod had about 30,000 people lose power due to the heavy, wet nature of the snow. Winds were also strong on the Cape and islands, gusting 45 to 55 mph in the afternoon which reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 4 to 11 inches of snow fell in Hartford County. This was the bullseye of the snow totals in southern New England. Some notable accumulations included 15.5 inches in Farmington, 15.2 inches in West Hartford, and 12.5 inches in East Windsor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure pushed northeast from the Tennessee Valley and moved along and off the coast. An accumulating snow took place across portions of Fairfield County, with far northern sections towards the Litchfield border receiving heavy snow in the vicinity of New Fairfield. Further south and east where the snow mixed with, and even changed to rain amounts were much lower.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow amounts varied greatly across Northern Fairfield with Trained spotters reporting 8.5 inches in New Fairfield, 8.0 inches at 4 SSE Sherman, and 4.4 inches at Danbury. However, amounts were noticeably lower going south with Redding reporting 4.0 inches, Stamford reporting 3.5 inches, Norwalk reporting 3.0 inches, and a Cocorahs report of 2.6 inches in Brookfield. Well more than half the zone reported snow that fell short of warning criteria with some amounts on the order of 1 to 3 inches along the immediate sound shore with Bridgeport NWS observation of 3 .0 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An accumulating snow fell across Northern New Haven County with 5 inch totals reported at 1 N Cheshire and Middlebury by the public, and 4.6 inches in Waterbury and 4.0 inches in Hamden from Cocorahs. Amounts further south ranged from under an inch along the Sound shore, to most 1 to 3 inches across the southern part of Northern New Haven County.

Trained spotters reported 6.5 inches in Cromwell and 3.9 inches at 3 WNW Cromwell.  Cocorahs reported 3.2 inches in Durham, 3.0 inches in Middlefield, and 1.9 inches in Higganum. Amounts decreased sharply from here going south and southeast. Amounts averaged well below warning criteria across Northern Middlesex County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong storm moved up the east coast passing near the 70/40 benchmark brought heavy snow to southern New England beginning late Monday night and continuing on Sunday. The first round of heavy snow fell overnight followed by a lull in the snow and mixing with rain Sunday morning. Comma head snow then filled back into eastern MA and RI bringing moderate to heavy snow Sunday afternoon and evening. Snow amounts ranged from 2 to 18 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 7 to 9 inches of rain fell. Some accumulations included 8.5 inches in Columbia, 8.4 inches in Staffordville, and 8 inches in Tolland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave coupled with modest instability, strong shear, and steep mid level lapse rates resulted in several severe thunderstorms from western MA to eastern CT to RI to southeast MA. All the severe reports were large hail reports. The cool marine layer prevented any wind damage and tornado risk. The largest hail stone to fall was 2 inches in Narragansett, RI.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Pomfret 1.5 inch hail was reported. In Killingly and Danielson 1 inch hail was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong area of low pressure shifted northeast through the eastern Great Lakes Region on January 13, 2024. This sent a warm front, quickly followed by a cold front, across Southern Connecticut during the morning hours. The resulting strong pressure gradient helped produce high winds across the area during this period.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A measured high wind gust of 62 mph occurred at a mesonet station at Stonington Outer Breakwater, CT at 704 AM Local Standard Time. In addition, a sustained wind of 40 mph was measured at ASOS station KGON (Groton Airport) at 640 AM Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two rounds of storms moved through southern New England causing sporadic wind damage mainly in northwestern and north central Massachusetts but also in Connecticut to a lesser extent.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Pomfret, a very large tree limb 20 inches in diameter was down on Kearney Rd bringing down several wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough approached from the north, with the region in the front left quadrant of an upper level jet streak. With warm, humid air at the surface, SBCAPE values were able to climb to around 2000 J/kg. Storms fired up and some were able to become strong to severe and produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple reports and pictures from Wallingford of 1.00 inch to 1.50 inch hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A swift-moving, coastal low pressure system ejected out of the Mid-Atlantic states and tracked east-northeast adjacent to the New England coastlines. Highest snowfall amounts fell in the southeast portions of Litchfield County, leading to a steep, southeast to northwest snowfall gradient. Therefore, while moderate to heavy snow (6-11 inches) fell in the southern portions of the county, light to moderate snow (<1 - 5 inches) fell across the north.||Key Impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 5.7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure shifted northeast through the eastern Great Lakes on January 13, 2024, sending a warm front through during the morning hours, followed quickly by a cold front. Strong winds produced by this system, combined with peaking high astronomical tides associated with a recent new moon, produced widespread moderate coastal flooding along the southern Connecticut coastline.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding occurred at the New Haven NOS tidal gauge, with water levels above the moderate threshold of 9.3 ft MLLW from 1054 AM EST to 112 PM EST, and a peak water level of 10.1 ft MLLW from 1148 AM EST to 1230 PM EST. ||Moderate coastal flooding occurred at the nearby Bridgeport CT NOS tidal gauge, with water levels above the moderate threshold of 10.2 ft MLLW from 1106 AM EST to 106 PM EST, and a peak water level of 10.7 ft MLLW from 1142 AM EST to 1224 PM EST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate coastal flooding occurred at the New London NOS tidal gauge, with water levels above the moderate threshold of 6.0 ft MLLW from 854 AM EST to 1148 AM EST, and a peak water level of 6.6 ft MLLW from 1000 AM EST to 1112 AM EST.

A strong storm system brought gusty winds, heavy rainfall, river flooding and a light accumulation of high elevation wet snow to northwestern Connecticut from the afternoon hours on January 9 into the early morning hours on January 10, 2024. The main impact from this system was the heavy rainfall as widespread 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across Litchfield County. This resulted in flooding along the Still and Housatonic rivers with moderate to major flooding occurring along portions of the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Housatonic River at Stevenson Dam rose above moderate flood stage during the late evening hours on January 9 into the afternoon hours on January 10. This resulted in numerous homes in the Maples area of Shelton along Indian Well Road to be affected by flooding.

The main impact from this system was the heavy rainfall as widespread 2 to 4 inches of rain fell across Litchfield County. This resulted in flooding along the Still and Housatonic rivers with moderate to major flooding occurring along portions of the river.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Still Water at Brookfield rose above major flood stage during the early morning hours on January 10 to the late evening hours on January 10. This resulted in the river flooding roads and parking lots as well as some buildings along the river.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The complex interaction of a northern stream shortwave and a southern stream coastal low pressure system brought a mix of snow, freezing rain, and plain rain to northwest Connecticut from the evening of Friday, March 22 through Saturday evening, March 23, 2024. While Litchfield County did not receive significant snowfall totals as a whole, the northern half of the county received amounts between 1-3 inches with the southern half generally received less than an inch. However, freezing rain accumulations around one tenth of an inch and heavy rainfall amounts ranging from about 1-3 inches contributed to nearly 400 people losing power in Litchfield County courtesy of downed trees and power lines. The towns impacted the most include Kent and New Milford.||Key Impacts: Power outages, school closures, traffic accidents, travel delays.

A moisture rich, strong low pressure system brought rain, light snow and gusty winds to Northwest Connecticut from Saturday, March 9 through Monday, March 11, 2024. Generally, Litchfield County received less than one inch of snowfall. Rainfall amounts ranged from about one half inch to about 2 inches. Additionally, strong winds between 35 and 50 mph were reported. ||Key Impacts: travel delays.

Low pressure across the Tennessee Valley and the Gulf states tracked tracked north, and redeveloped and intensified rapidly off the Mid Atlantic coast. A wet snowfall occurred across Southern Connecticut, with heavy wet snow across the western half of Southern Connecticut and interior Eastern Southern Connecticut mostly away from the immediate coast. Lesser amounts were reported across southeastern portions mainly due to marginal surface temperatures which precluded higher snow amounts in some urban locations and further east.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy wet snow fell across Southern Middlesex, especially away from the immediate coast. A trained spotter over in Madison on the border of Southern New Haven and Southern Middlesex reported 8.6 inches. Running just along the northern edge of the zone 2.7 WNW Chester Center reported 7.5 inches via Cocorahs, and 1 NNW Killingworth reported 7.0 inches, thus half the zone estimated to have reached warning criteria on a line oriented WSW to ENE running just away from the shoreline.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wet snow accumulated across Southern New London County, but warning criteria snow was met in consistent fashion just north of the northern boundary of the zone.  There was one report of 6.0 inches at 1 NNE New London, but it was the only report of 6 inches. Stonington only reported 1.2 inches as of 0900. Other coastal locations in New London County like East Lyme, Old Lyme, and Mystic which all reporting under 2 inches by mid morning did not report again.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A quick hitting winter storm brought around 12 hours of snow, centered over Connecticut, with the greatest amount of snow in Massachusetts falling on Cape Cod and the islands. This was a notoriously difficult to forecast storm passing south of 40/70 benchmark, which was a significant shift south in the final 24 hours leading up to the storm. This moved the band of heavy snow well south of Boston (0.1) to Cape Cod which received 7-9 inches of snow. The heaviest snow of the storm fell in Hartford County, Connecticut where over 15 inches of snow was reported. The result was many schools and businesses in eastern MA closing and receiving little to no snow. Cape Cod had about 30,000 people lose power due to the heavy, wet nature of the snow. Winds were also strong on the Cape and islands, gusting 45 to 55 mph in the afternoon which reduced visibility in blowing snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 5 to 12 inches of snow fell in Windham County. Some notable accumulations included 12 inches in Ashford, 11.5 inches in Pomfret, and 10.2 inches in Scotland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong cold front passed through the area during the evening of February 28th, 2024. This brought high winds behind its passage to Southern Connecticut during the early morning hours of February 29th, 2024.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large low pressure system impacted the area late on January 9th and into the 10th with long duration persistent moderate to heavy rainfall. In addition to widespread river flooding, some localized areas of flash flooding occurred as the heaviest rainfall associated with convection moved through. Precipitable water values of 1.2-1.4 inches were record levels for early January. The anomalous moisture and heavy rainfall resulted in a widespread 2 - 4 inches of rainfall, much of it occurring over a several hour timeframe before the cold frontal passage. The Yantic River reached Major Flood Stage on January 10th at 9Z and continued to remain in Major flood stage until 2330Z.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Yantic River remained in Major Flood Stage with a reported partial dam break in Fitchville as a result of heavy rain and snow melt.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area started off in the warm sector with an approaching cold front and pre-frontal trough from the west. Diurnal heating led to destabilization, with SBCAPE values reaching 2000-3000 J/kg. Thunderstorms fired up along the approaching pre-frontal trough and moved into the area. Given 40 knots of deep-layer shear, some storms were able to organize and become severe. The storms produced damaging winds across southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed tree limbs reported by Middletown PD. Some roads include West Field, East Street and Yellow Birch. One report included tree limb on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms moved through southern New England overnight bringing a line of very strong winds which caused damage in Connecticut while also producing two tornadoes in Rhode Island and Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At Bradley Airport the ASOS Station (KBDL) gusted to 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong storms moved through southern New England overnight bringing a line of very strong winds which caused damage in Connecticut while also producing two tornadoes in Rhode Island and Massachusetts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Danielson, there was a wind gust to 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area started off in the warm sector with an approaching cold front and pre-frontal trough from the west. Diurnal heating led to destabilization, with SBCAPE values reaching 2000-3000 J/kg. Thunderstorms fired up along the approaching pre-frontal trough and moved into the area. Given 40 knots of deep-layer shear, some storms were able to organize and become severe. The storms produced damaging winds across southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down near Ridgebury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level shortwave trough approached from the north, with the region in the front left quadrant of an upper level jet streak. With warm, humid air at the surface, SBCAPE values were able to climb to around 2000 J/kg. Storms fired up and some were able to become strong to severe and produce large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture of 1.25 inch hail from social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving cold front allowed for widespread thunderstorm development on June 30th. MUCAPE values of 2000-3000 J/kg with PWATs near 2 allowed for thunderstorms to produce heavy rainfall with rainfall rates of 1-2 inches per hour. These thunderstorms moved through the Lower Hudson Valley and Connecticut resulting in 1 to 2 inches of rain, most of which fell over a one to two hour period. This resulted in localized flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Danbury Emergency Management reports West Street is closed due to flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A two day stretch of dangerous heat and humidity occurred on July 9-10, 2024 across northwest Connecticut. Heat index values (or feel-like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across Litchfield County.

A series of mid-level shortwaves approached an environment characterized by 1000-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE and marginal shear (mainly at or under 30 kt of Effective Bulk Shear). This allowed isolated severe thunderstorms to develop and produce damaging wind gusts across southern CT.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and wires down on Inwood Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A round of thunderstorms brought wind damage and flash flooding to Connecticut.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In Hebron, a tree was down on wires.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad positively tilted upper-level trough approached from the west along with associated surface cold front and pre-frontal trough. The pre-frontal trough moved into an area with high instability (1750-2000 J/kg of MLCAPE). This combined with Effect Bulk Shear values around 25 kt allowed for multiple severe thunderstorms to develop. The storms were able to produce damaging wind gusts across southern CT, with DCAPE values around 750-1000 J/kg.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple trees down with wires at several locations including Golden Hill Road and Edgewood Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions began on June 18, 2024 and continued into June 20, 2024 across northwestern Connecticut. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region each day. Heat continued for additional days in some locations after June 20.

A multi-day stretch of hot and humid conditions began on June 18, 2024 and continued into June 20, 2024 across northwestern Connecticut. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across the region each day. Heat continued for additional days in some locations after June 20.

A cold front tracking south east across northwest Connecticut brought scattered showers and thunderstorms, some of which became severe in Litchfield County. Severe thunderstorms resulted in numerous reports of downed trees and wires, in addition to nearly 500 people without power. Additionally, heavy rain resulted in an isolated instance of flash flooding wherein Route 199 in Roxbury was washed out in the town of Washington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was downed on and partially blocking Route 7 near Beebe Hill Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 21, 2024 was the fourth day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across Litchfield County.||A lingering surface trough and weak cold front tracked southward through northwest Connecticut on June 21. Warm, moist antecedent conditions aided in providing a fairly unstable environment and requisite values of several severe weather parameters working in tandem with the aforementioned forcing mechanisms drove scattered severe thunderstorms in Litchfield County.||Severe thunderstorms resulted in several reports of downed trees. Additionally, one severe thunderstorm produced a weak, EF0 tornado that touched down in the town of Harwinton. The tornado touched down along Spring Hill Road just before 5 PM and followed along a path of about 2 miles. With maximum estimated wind speeds of 85 mph and a path width of 250 yards, extensive tree damage was sustained with limbs broken and trees uprooted sporadically along the path. An estimated $15000 in tree damage was sustained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: June 22, 2024 was the sixth and final day in the stretch of hot, humid conditions. Heat index (or feels like temperatures) reached between 95 and 104 degrees across Litchfield County.||A low pressure system and potent upper shortwave tracked east from the Great Lakes region, lifting a warm front through Litchfield County on the morning of June 23. With a cold front to the west, northwest Connecticut fell into the warm sector of the system with warm, moist antecedent conditions contributing to sufficient instability. Higher values of low- and deep-layer shear and steep lapse rates paired with ample forcing from a low level jet gave way to scattered showers and thunderstorms during the afternoon of June 23. Some storms became severe, leading to a few reports of tree and wire damage in Litchfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were downed along Route 7 near Kent.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two trees were downed on Carmen Hill Road No. 2 near Candlewood Lake Road South near New Milford.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Spring Hill Road in Harwinton, CT on June 21, 2024 just before 5 PM. The tornado moved in a general southeast direction, but the path was curved and the tornado moved almost southward for a period before turning eastward. The tornado ended along Route 4 near the Harwinton Ambulance station. Most of the damage consisted of broken tree limbs with some uprooted trees. Damage was sporadic along the path. Video of the tornado was posted to social media by the general public.

A potent Miller Type B storm system (weakening low pressure to the west over the Great Lakes and strengthening low off the east coast) brought gusty winds, rain, and light snow to northwest Connecticut over the period of April 3-5, 2024. Snowfall totals generally ranged from less than 1 inch up to near 4 inches. ||Key Impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 2 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Picture of large tree down on home with broken deck near Candlewood Shores Road in Brookfield CT.

Some of these include a tree down on a power line at Federal Road and Del Mar Drive, a tree uprooted and on a house off Stony Road and a damaged car in a parking lot from a fallen tree.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure slowly approached from the Mid-Atlantic coast and passed just south and east of Long Island, NY. A tight pressure gradient around the low pressure center helped produce high winds over southern Connecticut on April 3, 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple instances of high sustained winds and wind gusts occurred at mesonet sites. High sustained wind reports include 47 mph in Greenwich at 215 PM, 46 mph 2 miles ENE of Stamford at 125 PM, 45 mph 2 miles S of Rowayton at an elevation of 67 feet at 1058 AM,  and 41 mph 1 mile ESE of Norwalk at 1028 AM. A high wind gust of 62 mph occurred at the same mesonet station 1 mile ESE of Norwalk at 848 PM. A different mesonet station, Norwalk Light Station, located 2 miles ESE of Norwalk, recorded a high wind gust of 61 mph at 1103 AM. All times listed are in Local Standard Time.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The region was placed on the northeast edge of an upper level ridge, with subtle convectively induced disturbances moving through. Thunderstorms were triggered in an environment with weak shear and moderate to strong instability. Some of these storms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four people injured by a lightning strike at a Voluntown campsite.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system led to prolonged periods of heavy rainfall in northwest Connecticut on August 18, 2024. Rainfall totals were greatest in southern Litchfield County where total accumulations ranged from 1.75 to 8.80. As a result, numerous instances of flash flooding were reported. Flash flooding led to numerous road closures, damaged roadways, and damaged business parking lots. Additionally, numerous home basements became flooded. The towns most impacted included Oxford, Roxbury, and Woodbury. The extent of flooding and damaged sustained thereafter, led to President Biden approving Connecticut Governor, Ned Lamont's request for Federal Emergency Declaration which included Litchfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was observed on Good Hill Road between Railtree Road and Transylvania Road with numerous cars stranded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A coastal low pressure system led to prolonged periods of heavy rainfall in northwest Connecticut on August 18, 2024. Rainfall totals were greatest in southern Litchfield County where total accumulations ranged from 1.75 to 8.80. As a result, numerous instances of flash flooding were reported. Flash flooding led to numerous road closures, damaged roadways, and damaged business parking lots. Additionally, numerous home basements became flooded. The towns most impacted included Oxford, Roxbury, and Woodbury. The extent of flooding and damaged sustained thereafter, led to President Biden approving Connecticut Governor, Ned Lamont's request for Federal Emergency Declaration which included Litchfield County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An overwhelmed storm drain resulted in water flowing over Painter Hill Road in the Town of Roxbury.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Hot, humid conditions were present in northwest Connecticut on Friday, August 2, 2024. Heat index values (feels-like temperatures) reached the mid to upper 90s in Litchfield County, creating dangerously hot and humid conditions.

Hot, humid conditions were present in northwest Connecticut on Friday, August 2, 2024. Heat index values (feels-like temperatures) reached the mid to upper 90s in Litchfield County, creating dangerously hot and humid conditions.

A frontal system over the area on August 18th allowed for the development of rain early in the morning. As the day progressed, mesoscale features including multiple boundary interactions and a mesolow allowed thunderstorms to continue to intensify with persistent back-building and training over the same areas in southern Connecticut. These thunderstorms produced anywhere from 3-4.5 inch per hour rainfall rates resulting in several considerable Flash Flood Warnings and a Flash Flood Emergency being issued. Numerous rescues, road and bridge washouts, and several mudslides resulted in widespread damage. Several large-stem rivers including the Naugatuck, Pomperaug, Housatonic, and Still Rivers all reached major flood stage at some point during the day as a result of a widespread 5-8 inches of rainfall with localized amounts of up to 12-13 inches. Unfortunately, 3 people lost their lives in Connecticut as a result of the flash flooding.||Preliminary damage assessment costs for Fairfield County is $5.8 million and New Haven County is $4 million. This is not a final number.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flood waters stranded multiple motorists on Oxford Road near the Brookside Inn. People  inside a nearby home and the restaurant are being rescued.

These thunderstorms produced anywhere from 3-4.5 inch per hour rainfall rates resulting in several considerable Flash Flood Warnings and a Flash Flood Emergency being issued. Numerous rescues, road and bridge washouts, and several mudslides resulted in widespread damage. Several large-stem rivers including the Naugatuck, Pomperaug, Housatonic, and Still Rivers all reached major flood stage at some point during the day as a result of a widespread 5-8 inches of rainfall with localized amounts of up to 12-13 inches. Unfortunately, 3 people lost their lives in Connecticut as a result of the flash flooding.||Preliminary damage assessment costs for Fairfield County is $5.8 million and New Haven County is $4 million. This is not a final number.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Portions of Old Waterbury Road being washed away due to flood waters.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A clipper system moved north of southern New England during the overnight and early morning hours bringing widespread light to moderate snow to the interior of southern New England.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Generally 1 to 6 inches of snow fell with localized higher amounts including 8.5 inches in Tolland and 8.2 inches in Staffordville. There was one report of wires down with a road blocked on Rolling Meadow Dr.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The area was situated on the western edge of an upper level low as it spun over the Canadian Maritimes. An embedded shortwave rotated around the low and helped spark thunderstorms in a very unstable environment. One of these storms became severe and produced damaging wind gusts and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree and power lines down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions across portions of northwestern Connecticut resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) across portions of Litchfield County starting on November 12, 2024 and continuing into December. On November 9, 2024, Governor Ned Lamont, per the recommendation of the Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup, declared a Stage 2 drought advisory for all of Connecticut. At this stage, residents and businesses were encourages to minimize unnecessary water usage. The Stage 2 Drought advisory continued through the month of December with severe drought remaining in place across much of northwestern Connecticut.

Abnormally dry conditions across portions of northwestern Connecticut resulted in the development of severe drought conditions (Severe - D2) across portions of Litchfield County starting on November 12, 2024 and continuing into December. On November 9, 2024, Governor Ned Lamont, per the recommendation of the Connecticut Interagency Drought Workgroup, declared a Stage 2 drought advisory for all of Connecticut. At this stage, residents and businesses were encourages to minimize unnecessary water usage. The Stage 2 Drought advisory continued through the month of December with severe drought remaining in place across much of northwestern Connecticut.

A somewhat surprise localized advisory level snow fell with marginal surface temperatures across far northern portions of Southern Connecticut from the late evening hours on December 4th, into the overnight hours of the early morning of December 5th. An upper level disturbance rotated around the base of a longwave trough as the primary surface low tracked well north of the region. Noticeably less to trace amounts of snow fell further south across Southern Connecticut closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 3.2 inches at 4 NNW New Fairfield, 2.5 inches at 1 E Danbury, and 2.4 inches at 3 WSW Shelton.  Cocorahs observers also reported 3.0 inches at Ridgefield, 2.8 inches 4.5 SSE Bethel, and 2.5 inches in Monroe.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat surprise localized advisory level snow fell with marginal surface temperatures across far northern portions of Southern Connecticut from the late evening hours on December 4th, into the overnight hours of the early morning of December 5th. An upper level disturbance rotated around the base of a longwave trough as the primary surface low tracked well north of the region. Noticeably less to trace amounts of snow fell further south across Southern Connecticut closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Public observers reported 4.5 inches in Wolcott and 2.9 inches at 2 SE Beacon Falls. Cocorahs observers reported 3.5 inches in Naugatuck and 2.5 inches in Waterbury. A trained spotter reported 3.3 inches in Seymour.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A somewhat surprise localized advisory level snow fell with marginal surface temperatures across far northern portions of Southern Connecticut from the late evening hours on December 4th, into the overnight hours of the early morning of December 5th. An upper level disturbance rotated around the base of a longwave trough as the primary surface low tracked well north of the region. Noticeably less to trace amounts of snow fell further south across Southern Connecticut closer to the coast.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported 5.5 inches in Lisbon and a Cocorahs observer reported 2.2 inches in Norwich. A few hundred power outages were reported across interior New London from weighted wet snow and wind. More power outages were reported just north of the CWA border, this information was passed along to us by DESPP / DEMHS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An accumulating and advisory level snowfall occurred across northern portions of Southern Connecticut in front of a departing strong high pressure system at 1048+mb and a weakening mid-level disturbance which resulting in overrunning. There was an expectation that less snow would be observed with less liquid precipitation overall and some freezing rain towards or just after precipitation onset. Snowfall amounts in far northern sections was more than anticipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters reported 4.0 inches at 4 NNW New Fairfield and 2.7 inches at 2 ESE Danbury.  Cocorahs observers reported 3.0 inches at Bethel and 2.7 inches at Brookfield. Light freezing rain amounts were also reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An accumulating and advisory level snowfall occurred across northern portions of Southern Connecticut in front of a departing strong high pressure system at 1048+mb and a weakening mid-level disturbance which resulting in overrunning. There was an expectation that less snow would be observed with less liquid precipitation overall and some freezing rain towards or just after precipitation onset. Snowfall amounts in far northern sections was more than anticipated.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Cocorahs observers reported 3.0 inches at Naugatuck, 2.0 inches in Meriden, and 1.8 inches in Hamden. Light amounts of freezing rain (0.02 inches) was also detected by the Meriden ASOS, and 0.03 inches at the New Haven ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent clipper low pressure system impacted northwest Connecticut late Wednesday, December 4 through Friday December 6, bringing widespread light to moderate snowfall and gusty winds. Snowfall totals ranged from 3 to 5 inches with locally higher amounts up to 6 inches. ||Key Impacts: travel delays.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 5.3 inches.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Zone average snowfall of 5.1 inches.
